title: practical english usage - Part 304
content:
Great Britain and Northern Ireland together are called the United Kingdom; Scotland and Wales; British is used for the people of these three countries.

(Great) Britain is normally used to mean the island which  includes England, _  and   England               ______________ 411 Britain,      the    United        Kingdom,        the    British      Isles Which is better: a good upbringing and a bad education, or the opposite?

(,soɪ  Theirkids are very-badly-educated-.. .) Their kids are very badly brought up - always screaming and fighting.

Lucy was brought up by her aunt and educated at the local school.

intellectual and other training that people get at school and university.

training that children receive at home. Educate and education are used for the Bring up and the noun upbringing are mostly used for the moral and social 410 bring      up   and   educate                   ___ For other uses of take, ►    602.

The  difference between come and go is similar. ►    424 Let's go and see Aunt May on Sunday. We can bring a picnic.

Americans often use bring where British English has take.

5 American      English He heard nothing for months. Then one day his brother brought him  a letter.

happens in stories.

bring for movements to the place where he/she is (or was or will be). This often hearer), that person can become the centre of our attention. In that case, we  use Sometimes when we  are talking about somebody else (not the speaker or 4 somebody        else's    position bring Susie?

I'm taking the kids to the circus tonight. Would you like to come with us and hearer’ s, even if take is used for the movement itself.

Bring (with) can be used to talk about joining a movement of the speaker’ s/ 3 joining     a  movement Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  412   broad and wide Nobody but her would do a thing like that. (More formal: Nobody but she. . .) (/, he, etc) are possible in a more formal style before a verb.

After but, we  usually use object pronouns (me, him,  etc). Subject pronouns 2 pronouns       after    but I looked under the bed, and what should I find but the keys I lost last week?

I walked out of the station, and who should I see but old Holly?

appearances, meetings, etc).

Note also the structure who/what should... but (used to talk about surprising But for the storm, I would have been home before eight.

I would have been in real trouble but for your help.

But for expresses the idea 'if something had not existed/happened’ .

I was last but two in the race yesterday.

Jackie lives next door but one. (= two houses from me) Note the expressions next but one, last but two, etc (mainly BrE).

I've finished all the Jobs but one.

He eats nothing but hamburgers. Everybody's here but George.

everybody, nothing, nobody, anywhere, etc).

We   use but to mean ‘ except’  after all, none, every, any, no (and everything, 1  use 413 but    meani    ng   'except'   _________ 37 ɪ but,    although,         though       and   however         * For other common expressions with broad and wide, see a good dictionary.

a wide variety/range (of opinions, etc) broad-minded (- tolerant)   broad daylight (= full, bright daylight) broad agreement (= agreement on most important points) Both words can express more abstract meanings. Common expressions: 2 abstract      meanings not The river is about half a mile wide, (     ... -wide haffamde.) Wide is used in expressions of measurement; note the word order.

Note also: broad shoulders; a broad back; wide eyes; a wide mouth.

She wore a simple green dress with a broad black belt.

Across the broad valley, the mountains rose blue and mysterious.

descriptions.

Broad can also be used in this physical sense, especially in more formal The old city has very wide streets. The car's too wide for the garage.

we  more often use wide.

To  talk about the physical distance from one side of something to the other, 1  physical      distance 412 broa     d  and   wide vocabulary «414     by: time You can rent a bicycle by the day or by the week.
